input.content:
Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 20. The new upper round bound is round 25; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 25; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Round 23 of 25 ###
Budget used so far: $485.86 / $1000.00

### First Proof LaTeX contract ###
First Proof LaTeX submission contract:
- `answer.tex` must be a complete standalone LaTeX document.
- Use exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The compiled PDF must be at most 12 pages.
- The `fullpage` package is permitted.
- Do not use any other margin/layout changes: no `geometry`, `a4wide`,
  `typearea`, `anysize`, manual `\textwidth`, `\textheight`,
  `\oddsidemargin`, `\evensidemargin`, `\topmargin`, etc.
- Do not change line spacing: no `setspace`, `\linespread`,
  `\baselinestretch`, `\onehalfspacing`, or `\doublespacing`.
- Do not change font size inside the document: no `\small`,
  `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize`.
- Run `pdflatex` and fix compile errors before claiming readiness.


### Workspace files ###
- answer.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-Anc1sCDRnuRHvtpCD7x4Ac-answer.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/answer.tex`.
- research_notes.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-Kbze1W1iietwFWB3NbgejH-research_notes.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/research_notes.tex`.
- references.bib: read from `/mnt/data/file-X4iLYZsJLNJyhG56qTpA2S-references.bib` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/references.bib`.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Verdict

The revision improves several presentation points and fixes the LaTeX-contract issues, but the proof is still **not answer-ready**. The main remaining problems are not cosmetic: several essential technical lemmas are still asserted with insufficient proof, especially the coefficient-boundary identification for \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\), the direct-sum/finite-fusion coefficient calculus, and the passage from coefficient products to normal-bidual support inequalities.

### LaTeX-contract check

I reconstructed the revised `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. It compiled successfully. The resulting PDF has **6 pages**. I found no prohibited margin, line-spacing, or in-document font-size changes. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`. Thus the LaTeX contract is satisfied.

### Literature checks

- DKEP Section 2.3 does contain the one-normal \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\) support construction for normal \(M\)-\(C^*\)-algebras, including the support formula for invariant subalgebras; DKEP Proposition 2.10 records the simultaneous-normal version as an inclusion/equality statement in \(M(A)^{**}\). This supports part of the revised support-bookkeeping discussion, though the proof still packages several consequences too quickly. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) using all operators in \(e_BB(L^2M)e_B\cong B(L^2B)\), and Example 5.11 identifies the coefficient boundary for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence with that subalgebra boundary. The revised proof still needs an additional argument to pass from that \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence to the fused \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 has explicit hypotheses \(M,JMJ\subset M(X)\), \(N,JNJ\subset M(Y)\), and a cyclicity condition for vectors whose \(T_\xi\)-operators lie in \(YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\). The revised direct-sum lemma cites this theorem but still does not verify the needed cyclicity/closure details for arbitrary direct sums. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN does provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used by the draft, and Theorem 2.4 gives diffuseness/fullness/factoriality for join-irreducible graph products with state-zero unitaries. These final graph-product citations are substantially improved. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- DKEP Lemma 8.5 does support the normal-bidual central-state criterion and mentions the natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. The finite-boundary lemma is plausible, conditional on the earlier support-intersection lemma. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

## Section-by-section audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The draft explicitly adopts the standard interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This addresses the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. Support bookkeeping

This section is improved relative to the previous version. The one-normal support formula is now closer to DKEP Section 2.3, and the simultaneous-normal version is at least plausibly obtained through Proposition 2.10.

However, the proof still compresses a nontrivial construction into a citation. In particular, the statement

\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y)
\]

requires checking that the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) in \(A^{**}\) is the same support appearing under the simultaneous normal representation, and that the two normal structures interact exactly as claimed. DKEP gives the one-normal formula and then states a simultaneous inclusion result, but the equality in this exact corner form is not written out in the draft. This may be repairable, but in a self-contained proof it remains an essential lemma rather than a transparent citation.

The last assertion, \(q_X=pr_X\) for a boundary piece \(X\), is also too brief. The proof says DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Proposition 2.4 imply that functionals vanishing on \(X\) are precisely those vanishing on \(K_X(N)\). This is plausible, but the draft does not verify all hypotheses needed to conclude that \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is the **smallest** simultaneous strong bimodule generated by \(X\), nor does it spell out why this gives equality of the normal support projections of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\). Since the later intersection lemma depends directly on \(q_U=pr_U\), this is still an essential point.

### 3. Subalgebra coefficient boundary

The revision fixes one previous problem: it no longer relies on Hilbert-space rank-one operators generating all of \(B(L^2B)\). It correctly notes that DKEP Example 3.4 allows arbitrary \(S\in B(L^2B)\).

But the proof still does not establish the full lemma as stated. It verifies the formula

\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
=
T_\xi^*(T_{\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\bar\beta})T_\eta
\]

for bounded elementary vectors and concludes that “all coefficients” of \(\mathcal H_B\) lie in \(X_B\). This is a gap. The coefficient boundary of a correspondence is generated by coefficients of **all bounded vectors**, not only elementary bounded tensors. To pass from elementary tensors to arbitrary bounded vectors, one needs a density/closure argument with uniform boundedness of the associated \(T_\xi\)-operators, or an application of DKEP Theorem 5.10. The draft does not provide this.

This matters because the later proof uses this lemma to identify \(X_{M_U}\) with the coefficient boundary of \(L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), and then constructs approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) from finite-fusion coefficients. Without the full coefficient-boundary identification, the main support-intersection argument is not established.

### 4. Direct sums and finite fusions

The revision adds the missing multiplier hypothesis \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\). That addresses one prior objection.

However, the proof remains too compressed at a crucial point. It says that for a direct sum, finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic dense and then “applying Theorem 5.10 once more” gives the conclusion for all bounded vectors. DKEP Theorem 5.10 requires a cyclic set of bounded vectors satisfying a specific \(T_\xi\in YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\) condition, not merely that the summands are individually relatively compact. The draft does not verify the required cyclic set for the direct sum, nor the closure/uniform boundedness details needed to pass from finite-support vectors to arbitrary bounded vectors.

The finite-fusion coefficient algebra \(\mathcal C_U\) is also still asserted rather than proved. The statement that finite products of coefficients are finite sums of coefficients after inserting conjugate factors is standard, but in this context it is an essential lemma because it feeds directly into \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\). The draft should at least give a precise formula with conventions and explain how finite sums/direct sums are handled.

### 5. Boundary-support intersection lemma

This is the central technical lemma, and it still depends on unresolved inputs.

The CDHJKN fusion rule itself is valid and appropriately cited. The rule decomposes fusions over \(M_{V_2}\) as sums indexed by subsets of the intersection of the relevant labels. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is the operator-algebraic passage from coefficient products to support inequalities. The line

\[
hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)
\]

rests on the direct-sum/finite-fusion lemma and on the coefficient-boundary identification above. Since those are not rigorously proved, this step is not secure.

The limiting argument in the ordinary bidual is clearer than before, and the use of strong-* convergence of approximate units is a reasonable route. But it still assumes the unproved support formula \(q_U=pr_U\) for the original boundary \(X_{M_U}\), and it assumes the existence of approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) lying in the specified norm-closed coefficient cones. Both assumptions trace back to earlier underproved lemmas.

Thus the claimed inequality

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]

is still not rigorously established.

### 6. Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This lemma is mostly plausible. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) is well motivated, and DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion. The use of the compact-corner u.c.p. map \(\Theta_0\) is also consistent with DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The main caveat is that this lemma depends on the preceding palindromic product being genuinely in \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), which in this proof is supplied only by the still-unproved intersection lemma.

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This section remains plausible but not fully rigorous.

The AFP reduced-word commutator formula

\[
[p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B)
\]

is asserted after a brief explanation. A strict proof should check it on the vacuum, one-letter words, and longer reduced words. Similarly, the orthogonality claim

\[
q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0
\]

from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\) needs a complete reduced-word verification.

These are likely repairable gaps, not obviously false statements. But as written, the proof invokes several reduced-word facts without enough detail for a standalone rigorous solution.

### 8. Graph-product application

This part is much improved. The graph-product AFP decomposition is now cited, the graph combinatorics are correct, and the appeal to CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is appropriate. I also ran a small brute-force check of the graph combinatorics for graphs up to seven vertices; no counterexample was found to the local claims used in choosing \(w_1,w_2\).

The final nonamenability argument is acceptable: CDHJKN gives a diffuse full factor under the stated hypotheses, and a separable diffuse amenable finite factor would be the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor, which is not full. The CDHJKN full-factor statement is explicitly supported by Theorem 2.4. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the final application depends essentially on the relative Bass–Serre proposition, the coefficient-boundary lemmas, and the boundary-support intersection lemma. Since those are not fully proved, the final conclusion is not established.

## Summary of previous concerns

**Addressed or partly addressed:**
- LaTeX-contract compliance: fixed.
- Graph-product AFP citation: added.
- Nonamenability/factoriality citation: improved.
- Rank-one issue in the subalgebra coefficient argument: partly fixed by referring to arbitrary \(S\in B(L^2B)\).
- Missing multiplier hypotheses in Theorem 5.10 applications: partly fixed.

**Still remaining:**
- Full proof that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have identical simultaneous normal supports.
- Full proof that the fused correspondence \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary exactly \(X_B\).
- Direct-sum closure and finite-fusion coefficient calculus.
- The central support-intersection inequality \(q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}\).
- Detailed AFP reduced-word verification in the Bass–Serre proposition.

**New issue introduced or made clearer:**
- The direct-sum lemma now invokes DKEP Theorem 5.10 “once more” but does not verify the exact cyclic-vector hypothesis required by that theorem.

## Final assessment

The revised proof has a coherent strategy and may be repairable, but it still relies on several unproved essential technical lemmas. Therefore `answer.tex` does not yet fully solve the problem as a complete rigorous solution.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
(empty)

### Compute worker reply (if you commissioned one last turn) ###
(empty)

from pathlib import Path
answer = r'''\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cC}{\mathcal C}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible means not a non-trivial graph join; equivalently the complement
graph \(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the notation of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.
For a finite algebra \(N\), put \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*,
\]
the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual.  If \(X\) is an
\(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\) is DKEP's associated compact hereditary
algebra and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Set
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the absence of an \(N\)-central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\) restricting to the trace on \(N\).

\section*{Boundary supports and coefficients}

If \(Y\subset A_N\) is hereditary and invariant under the two multiplier
actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), let \(s(Y)\) denote the support projection of the
normal copy of \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) inside \(\cA_N\), supplied by DKEP
Proposition 2.10.

\begin{lemma}[support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
For such a hereditary subalgebra \(Y\),
\[
        (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=s(Y)\cA_Ns(Y),
\]
and every contractive approximate unit of \(Y\) converges ultra-strongly* to
\(s(Y)\) in \(\cA_N\).  If \(X\) is a boundary piece with
\(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\), then
\[
        s(X)=s(K_X(N))=q_X .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Propositions 2.9 and 2.10 realize the simultaneous normal biduals by the
universal simultaneous normal representation and identify the bidual of an
invariant subalgebra with the ultraweakly closed corner generated by that
subalgebra.  The identity of this corner is \(s(Y)\), and an approximate unit of
a hereditary C\(^*\)-algebra converges ultra-strongly* to the identity of its
normal closure in any non-degenerate representation.

It remains to compare \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\).  DKEP Proposition 2.4 says that a
functional in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) which vanishes on a subspace also vanishes on
the smallest subspace containing it which is strong both as an \(N\)-bimodule
and as a \(JNJ\)-bimodule.  DKEP Proposition 3.6, together with the preceding
Corollary 3.2 there, identifies this strong closure of a boundary piece \(X\)
with
\[
        K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline X^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
        =\overline{K_X(N)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}},
\]
and in particular every element of \(K_X(N)\) lies in that strong closure of
\(X\).  Thus the functionals in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) annihilating \(X\) are
exactly those annihilating \(K_X(N)\) (the converse is immediate from
\(X\subset K_X(N)\)).  Equal normal annihilators give equal support projections
in the corner description above.
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
The cyclic vector \(\zeta_B=\widehat 1\otimes_B\widehat 1\) has coefficient
\(T_{\zeta_B}^*T_{\zeta_B}=e_B\).

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra fusion]\label{lem:subalg}
The boundary piece \(X_B\) of DKEP Example 3.4 is hereditarily generated by the
positive coefficients of \(\cH_B\).  Moreover \(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\),
and every bounded coefficient of every direct sum of copies of \(\cH_B\) is in
\(K_{X_B}(N)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since \(e_B\) is the coefficient of \(\zeta_B\), and DKEP Example 3.4 defines
\(X_B\) as the hereditary algebra obtained from the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-translates
of \(e_B\mathbb B(L^2N)e_B\), \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by positive
coefficients of \(\cH_B\).

Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\).  DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the left coefficient
boundary of \(H\) with \(X_B\), while the right boundary is
\(\mathbb B(L^2B)\) (the case \(B\subset B\) in Example 3.4).  Thus, by DKEP
Theorem 5.10, \(H\) is mixing relative to
\(X_B\times \mathbb B(L^2B)\), and its conjugate is mixing relative to
\(\mathbb B(L^2B)\times X_B\).  The elementary bounded tensors
\(\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta\) are \(N\)-\(N\) cyclic in \(\cH_B\), and
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta}=T_{\bar\eta}T_\xi
        \in X_B\,\mathbb B(L^2N)\,X_B
\]
for the cyclic family supplied by Theorem 5.10.  Applying Theorem 5.10 again to
\(\cH_B\) gives \(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B,X_B}(N,N)\) for every bounded
\(\alpha\in\cH_B\); DKEP Proposition 5.9 then gives
\(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\in K_{X_B}(N)\).  For a direct sum, take the union of the
cyclic families in the summands, supported in one coordinate.  It is cyclic for
the direct sum and satisfies the same \(T_\xi\)-condition, so Theorem 5.10 and
Proposition 5.9 give the assertion for all bounded vectors in the direct sum.
Finally,
\(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes_BJ\xi\) gives the stated
self-conjugacy by traciality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite fusion calculus]\label{lem:fusion}
Let \(X\) be a boundary piece for \(N\), with \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\).  Finite
Connes fusions, conjugates, and arbitrary direct sums of \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondences whose bounded coefficients lie in \(K_X(N)\) again have all
bounded coefficients in \(K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), put \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), and let \(\cC_U\) be
the linear span of coefficients of finite direct sums of non-empty finite
fusions of \(H_U\) and \(\overline{H_U}\).  Then \(\cC_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra,
and \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit in the norm closure of
\(\{c^*c:c\in\cC_U\}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The first assertion is a direct use of DKEP Theorem 5.10.  For a direct sum,
the cyclic set required in condition (1) of that theorem is the union of the
corresponding cyclic sets in the summands.  For a fusion, the elementary
tensors from the cyclic sets are cyclic and satisfy
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\); since \(X\) is hereditary,
\(X\mathbb B(L^2N)X\subset X\), so condition (1) again holds.  Theorem 5.10
then gives \(T_\zeta\in K_{X,X}\) for every bounded vector, and DKEP
Proposition 5.9 gives the coefficient conclusion.

For the algebra calculation, if \(c_1=T_\xi^*T_\eta\) and
\(c_2=T_\alpha^*T_\beta\) are coefficients of finite fusions \(K,L\), then,
using \(T_{\bar\eta}=T_\eta^*\),
\[
 c_1c_2=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta,          \tag{2.1}
\]
where the two vectors are viewed in the finite direct sum
\((K\otimes_N\bar K\otimes_N L)\oplus L\).  Adjoints are handled by conjugating
the correspondence.  Hence \(\cC_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra.

By Lemma \ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by positive
coefficients of \(H_U\).  If \(s\) is a finite positive sum of such generators,
then \(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is in the hereditary algebra
generated by \(s\), and the net of these elements, over finite sums \(s\) and
\(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a contractive approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\).
Polynomial approximation to \(g_\varepsilon^{1/2}\), with zero constant term,
puts \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) in the norm closure of \(c^*c\)'s with
\(c\in\cC_U\).
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\), hence \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\).

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .                                  \tag{3.1}
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^m U_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule of
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} give
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3.2}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction gives that any finite fusion with
labels \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes as a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).

Let \(h\in\overline{\{c^*c:c\in\cC_W\}}\) and
\(k\in\overline{\{d^*d:d\in\cC_U\}}\).  For \(h,k\) before taking the norm
closures, formula (2.1) writes \(hkh\) as a finite sum of coefficients of
finite fusions whose labels are all \(W\)'s and \(U\)'s; by (3.2) these are
direct sums of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset I\).  Lemma \ref{lem:fusion} and
\(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\) then give
\[
        hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M).                                   \tag{3.3}
\]
Norm closedness gives (3.3) for the displayed closures.

Choose contractive approximate units
\(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and \(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\) from the above
positive cones.  By Lemma \ref{lem:support}, \(h_\alpha\to q_W\) and
\(k_\beta\to q_U\) ultra-strongly* in \(\cA_M\).  Since each
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\) lies in the normal corner \(q_I\cA_Mq_I\), and
this corner is ultra-strongly closed, first letting \(\beta\) and then
\(\alpha\) tend to infinity gives
\(q_Wq_Uq_W\in q_I\cA_Mq_I\).  The left hand side is a positive contraction, so
it is \(\le q_I\).  The palindromic assertion follows by induction from
(3.1).
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{4.1}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and put \(q=q_i\).
Since \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\); hence \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\).
If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), compression by \(q^\perp\) gives an \(N\)-central
state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  Its restriction to \(N\) is a non-zero
central normal positive functional, hence a scalar multiple of \(\tau\), which
contradicts relative proper proximality.  Thus \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for all
\(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state on \(\cA_N\).  In the GNS representation each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, so \(\omega(R)=1\).  Since (4.1) is a positive
contraction in the corner \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\), \(0\le R\le q_0\); hence
\(\omega(q_0)=1\).  DKEP Lemma 8.5 supplies the canonical \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\cA_Nq_0,\qquad
        \Theta_0(x)=xq_0\quad (x\in N).
\]
Then \(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is an \(N\)-central state on
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\) with \(\psi|_N=\tau\), i.e. a Connes hypertrace.  This
contradicts non-amenability of the finite factor \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal form
is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{n\ge1,\ i_1\ne\cdots\ne i_n}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n},\quad i_j\in\{P,Q\}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) be the projections onto the summands whose first letter is
\(P\), respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  If \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first letter except that \(L^2B\) is sent
to a one-letter \(P\)-word and a one-letter \(P\)-word may acquire an
\(L^2B\)-component.  Hence, checking the three classes \(L^2B\), first-letter
\(P\), and first-letter \(Q\),
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{5.1}
\]
The two terms in (5.1) are \(N,JNJ\)-translates of \(e_B\), hence lie in
\(X_B\).  Interchanging \(P\) and \(Q\) gives the analogous formula for
\(x\in Q\ominus B\), and elements of \(B\) commute with \(p,q\).  Since these
subspaces generate \(N\), DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) whose restriction to
\(N\) is normal.  For \(\eta\in qL^2N\), left multiplication by a centered
element of \(P\) prepends a \(P\)-letter, so \(w_iqL^2N\subset pL^2N\).  Moreover
\(w_1^*w_2\in P\ominus B\), and left multiplication by such an element sends
first-letter \(Q\) words into first-letter \(P\) words; hence
\(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\).  Therefore the ranges \(w_1qL^2N\) and \(w_2qL^2N\) are
orthogonal and
\[
        w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p .
\]
Likewise \(a\in Q\ominus B\), so \(a p a^*\le q\).  Centrality gives
\(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)\), whence \(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power of \(h\) is an
alternating reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, so
\(E_B(h^n)=0\) for \(n\ne0\).  Thus \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal
and all have the same \(\varphi\)-value; hence \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  But then
\[
        1=\varphi(1)=\varphi(e_B+p+q)=0,
\]
a contradiction.  By DKEP Theorem 6.2, \(N\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph-product reduced-word
model gives the standard amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{6.1}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then
connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) give \(s\in L_v\) with
\(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both cases the
graph-product reduced-word calculus gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (6.1), so
Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_i L_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} puts the associated
palindromic product in \(q_\emptyset\cA_Mq_\emptyset\).  But
\(M_\emptyset=\C\), so \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor.  A diffuse
amenable finite factor is the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor and is not full, so
\(M\) is non-amenable.  It is separable because the graph is finite and the
vertices are separable.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the finite family
\(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{DKEP}
C.~Ding, S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and J.~Peterson,
\emph{Properly proximal von Neumann algebras},
Duke Math. J. \textbf{172} (2023), 2821--2894.

\bibitem{CDHJKN}
I.~Charlesworth, R.~de Santiago, B.~Hayes, D.~Jekel,
S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and B.~Nelson,
\emph{On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras},
Publ. Res. Inst. Math. Sci. \textbf{61} (2025), 713--762.

\bibitem{CF17}
M.~Caspers and P.~Fima,
\emph{Graph products of operator algebras},
J. Noncommut. Geom. \textbf{11} (2017), 367--411.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}
'''

 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0,
